title: Analysis of Vascular Permeability by a Modified Miles Assay
author: Hilda Vargas-Robles
license_note: PMC full-text XML — Bio-protocol — CC-BY confirmed in article XML
materials: Milli-Q water (Millipore, obtained using a Milli-Q reference device) Solutions
Prepare the 2% Evans blue dye solution (see Recipes).
Prepare the sterile histamine solution (see Recipes).
Evans blue 2%, intraperitoneal (IP); 0.5%, intravenous (IV) (Merck, catalog number: E
Ketamine hydrochloride 100 mg/mL (Aranda, catalog number: Q-0449-
Sodium phosphate dibasic anhydrous (Na 2 HPO 4 ) (J.T. Baker, catalog number: 3828-
Potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH 2 PO 4 ) (J.T. Baker, catalog number: 3252-
Insulin syringes, 27G × 13 mm gauge (G) needles, 1 mL (BD Plastipak, catalog number:
1,000 μL pipette tips (Axygen, catalog number: T-1000-B)
200 μL pipette tips (Axygen, catalog number: T-200-Y)

steps.title: Male, 8–10-week-old C57BL6/J and BALBC mice...
steps.instruction: Male, 8–10-week-old C57BL6/J and BALBC mice (animal facility of Cinvestav-IPN) Reagents

steps.title: Xylazine 20 mg/mL (PROCIN ® , PiSA, Reg Q-
steps.instruction: Xylazine 20 mg/mL (PROCIN ® , PiSA, Reg Q-

steps.title: Phosphate buffered solution (PBS) 1× Reagent...
steps.instruction: Phosphate buffered solution (PBS) 1× Reagent Final concentration Quantity or Volume NaCl 138 mM 8.06 g KCl 3 mM 0.22 g Na 2 HPO 4 8.1 mM 1.15 g KH 2 PO 4 1.5 mM 0.20 g Milli-Q H 2 O n/a 1,000 mL Total n/a 1,000 mL

steps.title: Anesthesia solution [IP dosing amount (12
steps.instruction: Anesthesia solution [IP dosing amount (12.5 μL/g mouse weight)] Reagent Final concentration Quantity or Volume Ketamine hydrochloride 100 mg/kg 150 μL Xylazine 13 mg/kg 75 μL Saline solution n/a 1,275 μL Total n/a 1,500 μL

steps.title: Evans blue 2% and 0
steps.instruction: Evans blue 2% and 0.5% Reagent Final concentration Quantity or Volume Evans blue 2% (w/v) 0.5% (w/v) 2 g 0.5 g 1× PBS n/a 100 mL Total n/a 100 mL

steps.title: Histamine solution Reagent Final concentration Quantity...
steps.instruction: Histamine solution Reagent Final concentration Quantity or Volume Histamine 20 ng/μL (w/v) 1,000 ng PBS 1× n/a 50 μL Total n/a 50 μL

steps.title: Alcohol 70% Reagent Final concentration Quantity...
steps.instruction: Alcohol 70% Reagent Final concentration Quantity or Volume Ethyl alcohol 70% (v/v) 70 mL Milli-Q H 2 O n/a 30 mL Total n/a 100 mL Laboratory supplies

steps.title: Transfer the solution into a 20...
steps.instruction: Transfer the solution into a 20 mL syringe and filter it through a 0.22 μm syringe filter. Keep the solution at room temperature.

steps.title: Use an insulin syringe with a...
steps.instruction: Use an insulin syringe with a 27G × 13 mm needle and fill with 2% Evans Blue dye at 4 mL/kg mouse body weight. Critical: Carefully remove all bubbles in the syringe.

steps.title: Hold the mouse gently by its tail
steps.instruction: Hold the mouse gently by its tail. Then, with your free hand, gently grab its neck and turn the mouse to expose the abdomen ( Figure 1A ). Figure

steps.title: Evans blue intraperitoneal administration
steps.instruction: Evans blue intraperitoneal administration. (A) Holding the mouse. (B) Cleaning the abdominal area with ethyl alcohol. (C) Evans blue inoculation.

steps.title: Clean the abdomen with tissue soaked...
steps.instruction: Clean the abdomen with tissue soaked in 70% ethanol where the injection will be applied ( Figure 1B ). Note: See the following video for administration guidelines: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=s9skgg7dHIA&t=712s

steps.title: Insert the needle at a 45° angle. (Optional
steps.instruction: Insert the needle at a 45° angle. (Optional: Aspirate a little to ascertain that you are in the peritoneum.)

steps.title: Slowly administer the Evans blue dye ( Figure 1C )
steps.instruction: Slowly administer the Evans blue dye ( Figure 1C ).

steps.title: Return the animal to its cage...
steps.instruction: Return the animal to its cage and let the dye absorb for 3 h. Note: After 3 h, the footpads, nose, and ears must appear slightly blue indicating successful injection ( Figure 2 ). The mouse is now ready for the permeability assay. Figure

steps.title: The BALB/C mouse appears slightly blue...
steps.instruction: The BALB/C mouse appears slightly blue after 3 h of Evans blue dye administration. (A) General view of the mouse with slightly blue coloration. (B) Mouth and foot pads appear blue after Evans blue injection. (C) Ears appear slightly blue after Evans blue injection. B. Evans blue intravenous (IV) administration (0.5% Evans blue dye)

steps.title: Prepare the 0
steps.instruction: Prepare the 0.5% Evans Blue dye solution (see Recipes).

steps.title: Transfer the solution into a 20...
steps.instruction: Transfer the solution into a 20 mL syringe and filter it through a 0.22 μm syringe filter. Keep the solution at room temperature.

steps.title: Use a 27G × 13 mm...
steps.instruction: Use a 27G × 13 mm syringe and fill the syringe with 100 μL of 0.5% Evans blue dye per mouse. Carefully remove all bubbles in the syringe.

steps.title: Keep the mouse briefly (~5–10 min)...
steps.instruction: Keep the mouse briefly (~5–10 min) under an infrared lamp to dilate the tail veins. Note: If no infrared lamp is available, you can incubate the tail briefly in 37 °C water.

steps.title: Transfer the mouse gently into a...
steps.instruction: Transfer the mouse gently into a mouse restrainer to immobilize it.

steps.title: Clean the tail with 70% ethanol
steps.instruction: Clean the tail with 70% ethanol.

steps.title: Insert the needle carefully into the...
steps.instruction: Insert the needle carefully into the lateral vein and slowly administer the Evans blue dye solution ( Figure 3 ). Note: Intravenous injections are not easy and require practice. Make sure to hold the needle at a flat angle (see Figure 3 ) to enter the vein. If you sense any resistance while administering, you have not entered the vein correctly. The veins will be much easier to see in a BALB/C mouse ( Figure 3 ). Figure

steps.title: Evans blue injection into the tail vein
steps.instruction: Evans blue injection into the tail vein. (A) BALB/C mouse. (B) C57BL6/J mouse. Arrows show the lateral vein.

steps.title: Return the animal to its cage...
steps.instruction: Return the animal to its cage and let the dye circulate for 30–60 min. Note: Usually, after 30 min, the footpads, nose, and ears of the mouse will turn blue if the dye was injected properly. The mouse is now ready for the permeability assay ( Figure 4 ). Figure

steps.title: Evans blue body distribution
steps.instruction: Evans blue body distribution. (A) C57BL6/J mouse. (B) BALB/C mouse. Please note that an intravenous (IV) injection leads to a more intense blue coloration than the intraperitoneal (IP) injection (compare with Figure 2). C. Permeability assay

steps.title: Administer the anesthesia solution intraperitoneally as described above
steps.instruction: Administer the anesthesia solution intraperitoneally as described above. Note: Wait for 10 min and then monitor the pain reflexes of the mouse by pinching the paws with forceps.

steps.title: Turn the mouse and shave the...
steps.instruction: Turn the mouse and shave the back carefully with a veterinary clipper shaver (Figure 5A1, B1). Note: It is advised to wait 1 h after shaving or shaving the day before the experiment since shaving irritates the skin.

steps.title: Fill an insulin syringe with a...
steps.instruction: Fill an insulin syringe with a 27G × 13 mm needle with the 1,000 ng/50 μL histamine solution.

steps.title: Stretch the skin with your index...
steps.instruction: Stretch the skin with your index finger and thumb (Figure 5A2/B2 and A3/B3).

steps.title: Insert ~5 mm of the needle...
steps.instruction: Insert ~5 mm of the needle at a 45° angle carefully into the skin. Administer the histamine solution very slowly and continuously. Notes:

steps.title: The administration must be intradermal
steps.instruction: The administration must be intradermal. Successful intradermal injection is indicated by the formation of a skin bubble (Figure 5A4, A5 and B4, B5). If a bubble does not appear, you have likely injected subcutaneously and this injection site must be aborted. Intradermal injection is characterized by strong resistance when inserting the needle. If the needle moves without resistance, it is most likely a subcutaneous injection.

steps.title: Repeat injections in the same mouse...
steps.instruction: Repeat injections in the same mouse if you want to test different stimuli by always applying 50 μL of each substance approximately 1–2 cm away from another injection site (up to 6 injections are possible per mouse back).

steps.title: Return the mouse to its cage and wait 30 min
steps.instruction: Return the mouse to its cage and wait 30 min.

steps.title: After 30 min, euthanize the anesthetized...
steps.instruction: After 30 min, euthanize the anesthetized mouse by cervical dislocation or anesthesia overdose (triple dose of that mentioned above).

steps.title: Make an incision in the lower...
steps.instruction: Make an incision in the lower skin using surgical scissors ( Figure 6A ). Figure

steps.title: Intradermal histamine injection into the shaved back skin
steps.instruction: Intradermal histamine injection into the shaved back skin. (A) C57BL6/J mouse. (B) BALB/C mouse. After correct intradermal injection, skin bubbles are visible (A4/A5 and B4/B5). For a detailed description of each procedure please see the text. Figure

steps.title: Skin cutting
steps.instruction: Skin cutting. (A) Make a transversal incision in the lower skin. (B) Kelly forceps are introduced between the skin layer and the muscular layer. (C) Once the skin is separated, it can be lifted and cut. Make sure to have good margins (~0.5 cm) along the injection sites.

steps.title: Separate the skin from the muscle...
steps.instruction: Separate the skin from the muscle and tissue using Kelly forceps ( Figure 6B ).

steps.title: Cut the entire back skin ensuring...
steps.instruction: Cut the entire back skin ensuring sufficient margins around the injection sites ( Figure 6C ).

steps.title: Then, cut ~1 cm circles to...
steps.instruction: Then, cut ~1 cm circles to recover the injected skin areas for dye extraction and quantification ( Figure 7 ). Note: The circles should be centered around the blue leakage as the injection site is not always clearly visible. Figure

steps.title: Evans blue leakage at the injection sites indicating vascular permeability
steps.instruction: Evans blue leakage at the injection sites indicating vascular permeability. (A) After Evans blue intraperitoneal (IP) administration in a BALB/C mouse. (B) After Evans blue IP administration in a C57BL/6J mouse. (C) After Evans blue intravenous (IV) administration in a BALB/C mouse. (D) After Evans blue IV administration in a C57BL/6J mouse. Please note that leakage is more intense after IV injection, thus making this the preferred route of administration. Yellow circles highlight the site of injection.

steps.title: Place skin samples in 1 mL...
steps.instruction: Place skin samples in 1 mL of dimethylformamide in a 2 mL microtube.

steps.title: Put the tube in an overhead...
steps.instruction: Put the tube in an overhead rotator at 40 rpm at room temperature for 24 h.

steps.title: Place 100 μL of dimethylformamide as...
steps.instruction: Place 100 μL of dimethylformamide as blank and 100 μL of each sample supernatant (extracted dye) in a cuvette or microplate and read the absorption spectrophotometrically at 620 nm.

steps.title: Take out the skin samples from...
steps.instruction: Take out the skin samples from the Eppendorf tube and dry the skin circles at room temperature for 2 days at room temperature on the workbench

description: Validation of protocol This protocol or parts of it has been used and validated in the following research articles: • Montoya-García et al. [5]. Arpin deficiency increases actomyosin contractility and vascular permeability. eLife ( Figure 8E ). • Moztarzadeh et al. [6]. Erk1/2 is not required for endothelial barrier establishment despite its requirement for cAMP-dependent Rac1 activation in heart endothelium. Arpin deficiency increases actomyosin contractility and vascular permeability. Tissue Barriers ( Figure 6A and B).
